title: US-003: JSON-RPC 2.0 Types
description:
Implement JSON-RPC 2.0 message types with serde_json::RawValue for opaque pass-through. This is the foundational protocol layer used by all gateways.

## Tech Stack
- serde + serde_json with RawValue
- IMPORTANT: serde_json::RawValue is a DST (like str). MUST use Box<RawValue> for owned values

## File: src/jsonrpc.rs

## Core Type: RawMessage
A flat struct is the wire type — NOT an enum. The same struct holds requests, responses, notifications, and errors. Classify via helper methods.

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct RawMessage {
    jsonrpc: String,                        // always "2.0"
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    id: Option<Box<RawValue>>,              // number, string, or null — preserved exactly
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    method: Option<String>,                 // present for requests/notifications
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    params: Option<Box<RawValue>>,          // opaque
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    result: Option<Box<RawValue>>,          // opaque
    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    error: Option<Box<RawValue>>,           // opaque (contains code, message, data)
}
```

IMPORTANT: Do NOT use `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]`. JSON-RPC messages may contain extension fields. Unknown fields must be silently preserved via `#[serde(flatten)] extra: HashMap<String, RawValue>` or simply dropped (dropping is acceptable for a transparent proxy).

## id Field Semantics
- `id: None` (field absent) → notification (no response expected)
- `id: Some(RawValue("null"))` → valid JSON-RPC id of null (response expected, response id will also be null)
- `id: Some(RawValue("42"))` → numeric id
- `id: Some(RawValue("\"abc\""))` → string id
Must distinguish None (absent) from Some(null). Use `#[serde(default, deserialize_with = "deserialize_optional_raw")]` or similar.

## Classification Helpers
```rust
fn is_request(&self) -> bool       // has id + method
fn is_response(&self) -> bool      // has id + (result or error), no method
fn is_notification(&self) -> bool  // has method, no id (id field absent, NOT id:null)
fn is_initialize_request(&self) -> bool // method == "initialize" && is_request()
fn method_str(&self) -> Option<&str> // convenience accessor
```

## Error Response Construction
Helper to build JSON-RPC error responses:
```rust
fn error_response(id: Option<Box<RawValue>>, code: i32, message: &str) -> RawMessage
```
Used by GatewayError (US-003a) and auto-init (US-011) to construct error bodies.
If id is None (notification that errored), use `RawValue::from_string("null")` for the response id.

## Batch Support
Batch = JSON array at top level. Deserialization strategy:
1. Try `serde_json::from_str::<RawMessage>(line)` first (common case, single message)
2. If that fails with "expected struct", try `serde_json::from_str::<Vec<RawMessage>>(line)`
3. If both fail → malformed JSON (log + discard for stdio, 400 for HTTP)
Alternatively: peek first non-whitespace byte. `{` → single, `[` → batch. This avoids double-parse.
Forward batch to child as a single line (re-serialize Vec<RawMessage> to one JSON line).

## Protocol Transparency Rules
- Unknown methods: forward without interpretation
- Arbitrary params/result shapes: forward as-is
- Arbitrary error.data: forward as-is
- Batch JSON-RPC requests (JSON arrays): forward to child as single line
- JSON-RPC notifications (no id): forward without awaiting response
- Server-initiated requests: forward from child/remote to client without interpretation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `RawMessage` struct (NOT enum) using `Box<RawValue>` for opaque pass-through of params/result/error
- [ ] id field: correctly distinguishes absent (None) vs null (Some(null)) vs value (Some(42), Some("abc"))
- [ ] `is_request()`, `is_response()`, `is_notification()`, `is_initialize_request()` helpers
- [ ] `error_response(id, code, message)` constructor helper
- [ ] No `deny_unknown_fields` — silently accept extension fields
- [ ] Batch support: detect and deserialize JSON arrays as `Vec<RawMessage>`
- [ ] Unknown methods: deserialize without rejection
- [ ] Round-trip tests: request, response, notification, error, batch, null id, string id, numeric id, unknown method, large params
- [ ] Test: absent id vs explicit null id are distinct (`is_notification()` returns true only for absent)
- [ ] Test: error_response() produces valid JSON-RPC error with correct structure

## Quality Gates
- `cargo +nightly build --release` — no warnings
- `cargo +nightly test` — all tests pass
- `cargo +nightly clippy -- -D warnings` — no warnings
notes:
PROTOCOL TRANSPARENCY INVARIANTS: The JSON-RPC types module MUST enforce these negative requirements across the project:
- MUST NOT reject messages based on MCP protocol version
- MUST NOT add/remove/modify any JSON-RPC fields (except id in WS multiplexer D-001)
- MUST NOT buffer/reorder messages except where explicitly specified (auto-init, WS correlation)
Use RawValue pass-through everywhere to guarantee this. These are testable invariants for the compat harness.

title: US-004: Line-Delimited Stdio Codec
description:
Line-delimited codec for reading/writing JSON-RPC messages over stdin/stdout pipes.

## Tech Stack
- AsyncBufRead/AsyncWrite from asupersync (or std BufReader wrapper)
- serde_json for parsing

## File: src/codec.rs

## Read Path (stdout)
1. Read lines from AsyncBufRead (newline-delimited)
2. Line separator: accept BOTH \n AND \r\n (Windows compat). Split on \n, strip trailing \r
3. Skip empty lines (whitespace-only)
4. Per-message size limit: 16MB. If a single line exceeds 16MB, log error and skip (do not crash)
5. Validate UTF-8: invalid UTF-8 on stdout is a FATAL protocol violation. Return error to caller (caller kills child/session, returns JSON-RPC -32603 to in-flight requests). Increment decode_errors counter. Do NOT use lossy decode for stdout.
6. Parse each line as JSON via serde_json::from_str::<RawMessage>
7. On parse failure: log at error level with first 200 chars of line, skip message
8. Partial line buffer limit: 64MB max (from PRD). If partial buffer exceeds 64MB, treat as protocol violation — kill child/session
9. Handle partial UTF-8 at chunk boundaries correctly (multi-byte codepoints split across reads must be buffered, not treated as invalid)

## Read Path (stderr) — Lossy Mode
Separate read function for stderr with lossy UTF-8 decode:
- Replace invalid UTF-8 bytes with U+FFFD (lossy decode)
- Never return fatal error for invalid UTF-8 on stderr
- Used by ChildBridge (US-005) for stderr reader task

## Write Path
1. Serialize RawMessage to JSON string (serde_json::to_string)
2. Append \n
3. Write to AsyncWrite
4. Flush after each message (critical for pipe buffering — child needs messages immediately)
5. Mutex-protected for shared-child modes (SSE, WS, stateful HTTP). The caller or a write wrapper must hold a mutex to prevent interleaved writes from concurrent request handlers.

## EOF Handling
If trailing buffer after EOF is valid JSON, emit as final frame. If invalid, log warning and discard.

## Global Memory Budget
The 64MB partial buffer limit is per-child. There is a 256MB global cap across all sessions (see US-005 notes). The codec itself enforces the per-child 64MB limit. The global semaphore is owned by ChildBridge (US-005) and passed in at construction.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `read_message()`: read one line, parse as RawMessage, return
- [ ] `write_message()`: serialize + newline + flush, mutex-protected for shared-child modes
- [ ] Line separator: handles both \n and \r\n (strip \r before JSON parse)
- [ ] Empty lines skipped
- [ ] Stdout invalid UTF-8: FATAL error returned to caller (not lossy decode). Caller kills child/session
- [ ] Stderr lossy decode mode: replace invalid bytes with U+FFFD, never fatal
- [ ] Per-message size limit: 16MB max per line. Lines exceeding limit are logged and skipped
- [ ] Partial buffer limit: 64MB max. Exceeded → return fatal error (caller kills child)
- [ ] Partial UTF-8 at chunk boundaries: buffered correctly (not treated as invalid)
- [ ] Parse errors: logged at error level with truncated preview, message skipped
- [ ] EOF handling: trailing valid JSON emitted as final frame; invalid trailing data logged and discarded
- [ ] Batch support: JSON array lines forwarded as-is (parsed as Vec<RawMessage> by caller, not codec)
- [ ] Unit tests: single message, batch, empty lines, \r\n, oversized message, parse error, partial buffer overflow, invalid UTF-8 on stdout (fatal), invalid UTF-8 on stderr (lossy), EOF with/without trailing newline
- [ ] criterion benchmark: parse 10k messages (criterion microbenchmark framework)

## Quality Gates
- `cargo +nightly build --release` — no warnings
- `cargo +nightly test` — all tests pass
- `cargo +nightly clippy -- -D warnings` — no warnings
notes:
IMPORTANT: Line separator must accept BOTH \n AND \r\n for Windows compatibility.
Split on \n, then strip trailing \r from each line before JSON parsing.

MUTEX OWNERSHIP: The codec write function does NOT hold a mutex internally. The caller (ChildBridge or gateway) wraps stdin writer in Arc<Mutex<StdinWriter>> and acquires the lock before calling write_message(). This is a Rust improvement over TS (D-012) which has no write serialization.

CONSTRUCTOR SIGNATURE: fn new(reader: R, max_buffer: usize) -> Self. The 64MB limit is enforced by the codec itself (per-child). The 256MB global cap is enforced externally by ChildBridge (US-005) via Arc<Semaphore>.

STDERR BUFFER: Stderr uses a separate read path with smaller buffer (64KB, not 64MB). Either expose a configurable buffer_size parameter on the codec, or provide a separate stderr_reader with its own limit. US-005 (ChildBridge) specifies "Stderr: 64KB buffer, 4KB line truncation."

DEV-DEPENDENCIES: Add criterion = "0.5" to [dev-dependencies] in Cargo.toml for codec benchmarks ("parse 10k messages" criterion benchmark).

BUFFER SIZE RELATIONSHIP: 64MB partial-line buffer (D-106) is for incomplete lines being accumulated. 16MB (D-101) is max per complete JSON-RPC message after line splitting. Overflow of either kills the child/session. These are independent limits at different codec stages.

CODEC OUTPUT TYPE: Codec yields RawMessage (newtype around Box<RawValue>) or Vec<u8>. Prefer Vec<u8> for zero-copy — let callers parse JSON only when needed. The codec is a line splitter, not a JSON parser.

title: US-005: Child Process Manager (ChildBridge)
description:
ChildBridge: spawn child MCP servers via `sh -c`, manage process groups with setsid/killpg, handle stdin/stdout/stderr as separate async tasks.

## Tech Stack
- Use `asupersync::process::Command` if available (per US-000 findings), else `std::process::Command` with async wrapper
- `nix` crate for setsid/killpg/waitpid
- Spawned inside Asupersync Region — Region cancel kills child

## File: src/child.rs

## Spawn Sequence
1. `Command::new("sh").arg("-c").arg(stdio_cmd)` with `pre_exec(|| { setsid(); Ok(()) })`
2. `stdin(Stdio::piped())`, `stdout(Stdio::piped())`, `stderr(Stdio::piped())`
3. `.take()` stdin, stdout, stderr into separate async tasks

## Stdout Channel Design (CRITICAL for deadlock prevention)
Stdout reader task writes to a bounded async channel (capacity 256 messages).
If channel is full (child producing faster than gateway consuming):
1. Log warning at debug level
2. Block the stdout reader task (natural backpressure to child via pipe buffer)
3. Do NOT drop messages
Gateway consumer tasks read from the receiver end. On child exit, stdout reader closes the sender.

## Stdout Invalid UTF-8 — FATAL
Invalid UTF-8 on stdout is a FATAL protocol violation (PRD line 335).
- Signal error to owning session/gateway
- Session/gateway must: close session, return JSON-RPC -32603 to in-flight requests
- Log the decode error, increment decode_errors counter
- Do NOT use lossy decode for stdout

## Kill Sequence
1. SIGTERM to process group (`killpg(pgid, SIGTERM)`)
2. Wait up to 5s for child exit
3. Check `waitpid(child_pid, WNOHANG)` before SIGKILL (PID reuse safety)
4. If still alive: SIGKILL to process group
5. Drain remaining stdout/stderr (up to 1MB, then truncate with log warning)
6. Close all FDs (stdin/stdout/stderr handles from .take() must be explicitly dropped)

## EPIPE Handling
- On broken stdin pipe: mark child as dead immediately, stop accepting new messages
- Propagate error to caller with transport-appropriate status (HTTP 502, WS close, SSE error event)
- Stateful sessions with dead child are terminally failed (no auto-restart — MCP session state lost)

## Stderr Handling
- Captured and logged at error level (all modes consistently)
- Buffer limit: 64KB per read. Lines >4KB truncated with `[truncated]` suffix
- Never queued — logged inline by stderr reader task (no channel, no buffering beyond per-read 64KB)
- Invalid UTF-8: lossy decode (replace with U+FFFD), do not crash (uses codec stderr lossy mode)

## Grandchildren
- `setsid` creates new process group. `killpg` sends signal to entire group
- Handles TS bug where `child.kill()` only kills `sh`, leaving `npm`/`node` orphaned

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Spawn via `sh -c` with `pre_exec(setsid)` for process group
- [ ] Use asupersync process Command if available, else std::process::Command with async wrapper
- [ ] Stdin/stdout `.take()`-ed into separate async tasks
- [ ] Stdout reader writes to bounded channel (256 capacity) — blocks on full, does not drop
- [ ] Stdout invalid UTF-8: FATAL error signaled to owning session/gateway (not lossy decode)
- [ ] `kill()`: SIGTERM to pgid -> 5s wait -> SIGKILL (with PID reuse safety check via waitpid WNOHANG)
- [ ] `Drop` impl sends SIGTERM to pgid (safety net)
- [ ] EPIPE detection: on broken stdin pipe, mark child dead, propagate error to caller
- [ ] Child exit detection: detect via wait status, propagate to owning session/gateway
- [ ] Stderr: read + log at error level, 64KB buffer, 4KB line truncation, lossy UTF-8 decode
- [ ] Spawned inside Region — Region cancel kills child
- [ ] Shutdown drain: up to 1MB stdout/stderr after kill, then truncate with log warning
- [ ] Integration test: spawn, read message, cancel Region, verify pgid killed within 2s
- [ ] Integration test: write to dead child stdin, verify EPIPE handling
- [ ] Integration test: rapid spawn/kill cycles (100+), verify no leaked pids or fds

## Quality Gates
- `cargo +nightly build --release` — no warnings
- `cargo +nightly test` — all tests pass
- `cargo +nightly clippy -- -D warnings` — no warnings
notes:
IMPORTANT: Global memory semaphore — 64MB max partial buffer per child, 256MB global cap across ALL sessions.
Implement as Arc<Semaphore> passed to each ChildBridge on spawn. New sessions rejected (503) when budget exhausted.
This prevents worst case 64MB x 1024 sessions = 64GB memory exhaustion.

PID 1 zombie reaping: NOT owned by this bead. Zombie reaping (SIGCHLD handler with waitpid(-1, WNOHANG)) is owned by US-009 (Signal Handling). ChildBridge only reaps its OWN child via direct waitpid(child_pid).

SHELL EXECUTION: Child command MUST be spawned through system shell: Command::new("sh").args(["-c", &stdio_cmd]). The --stdio value is a shell command string (e.g., "npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /"), not a bare executable path. All TS gateway modes use { shell: true }.

SPAWN SEQUENCE CAVEAT: Code snippets in description assume std::process::Command. If Asupersync provides asupersync::process::Command (per US-000 spike), adapt the pattern but preserve invariants: (1) new process group via setsid, (2) piped stdin/stdout/stderr, (3) kill_on_drop or equivalent.

REGION CANCEL MECHANISM: Spawn child management task inside a Region. When Region is cancelled, the task cx.checkpoint() returns Err(Cancelled), triggering kill sequence. Also implement Drop on ChildBridge as safety net to send SIGTERM.

CONSTRUCTOR: fn spawn(cmd: &str, global_mem_semaphore: Arc<Semaphore>, cx: &mut Cx) -> Result<ChildBridge, ChildError>

CHILD EXIT BEHAVIOR PER MODE (for callers):
- Shared-child modes (SSE, WS): exit gateway process with child exit code (or 1)
- Per-session modes (stateful HTTP): mark session failed, return 502 to that session, continue serving other sessions
- Stateless mode: return 502 for that request, continue serving
- Client modes: exit gateway with exit code 1

title: US-006: Session Manager
description:
Session manager for stateful Streamable HTTP mode. Manages session lifecycle, access counting, and timeout.

## Tech Stack
- Asupersync Region per session (Region cancel cascades to ChildBridge cleanup)
- DashMap or RwLock<HashMap> for session storage
- UUID v4 for session IDs (Mcp-Session-Id header) — cryptographically random, unguessable

## File: src/session.rs

## Session State Machine
```
(none) → Active → Closing → Closed
                    ↑
             (DELETE or timeout)
```
- Active: child alive, accepting requests
- Closing: received DELETE or timeout fired. Requests rejected with 503 (NOT 404). 5s drain timeout for in-flight requests, then Closed
- Closed: session removed from map, Region cancelled, child killed. Subsequent requests return 404 (session not found)

Note: A transient "Created" state may exist between session map insertion and child spawn completion. If child spawn fails, session transitions directly to Closed.

## Closing→Closed Drain Timeout
When entering Closing state:
1. Stop accepting NEW requests (return 503 with JSON-RPC -32000 for this session)
2. Allow in-flight requests up to 5s to complete
3. After 5s or all in-flight requests complete (whichever first), transition to Closed
4. Closed: cancel Region, kill child, remove from session map. Subsequent requests return 404.

## Access Counting (RAII Guard)
```rust
struct SessionAccessGuard { session_id: String, counter: Arc<AtomicU64> }
impl Drop for SessionAccessGuard { fn drop(&mut self) { self.counter.fetch_sub(1, Relaxed); } }
```
- Increment on request accept, decrement on response terminal state (via Drop)
- The guard must be held for the duration of the HTTP response lifecycle. Drop occurs when response reaches terminal state (body fully sent, connection closed, or error). NOT on handler function return — response may outlive handler (e.g., SSE streams)
- Must be cancellation-safe — Drop always runs, even on task cancel
- Count→0: start timeout timer. Timer holds Weak ref to session map (prevents keeping dead sessions alive)
- New request during active timeout: cancel timer, reactivate session
- Timer fires: check Weak ref still valid + access count still 0 before removing

## Cleanup Callback
```rust
type CleanupFn = Box<dyn Fn(SessionId) + Send + Sync>;
```
Invoked when sessions are removed, allowing gateway to clean up per-session resources.

## DELETE Handler
- Transition session to Closing state, return 200 OK (or 204 No Content)
- DELETE is idempotent: DELETE on already Closing/Closed returns success
- NOT 202 (PRD specifies 200/204)

## Max Sessions
Default: 1024 concurrent sessions. Enforced in session creation. Return 503 when limit reached.

## Global Memory Semaphore
256MB global memory budget shared across all sessions (see US-005). Session creation checks semaphore.

## Session ID Generation
Use `uuid::Uuid::new_v4()` — cryptographically random, unguessable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Session CRUD: create (with child spawn), get, delete
- [ ] State machine: (none) → Active → Closing → Closed
- [ ] Closing state: reject new requests with 503 + JSON-RPC -32000, drain in-flight up to 5s, then Closed
- [ ] Closed state: session removed from map, subsequent requests return 404
- [ ] DELETE: return 200/204, transition to Closing. Idempotent (DELETE on Closing/Closed returns success)
- [ ] Access counting with RAII guard (Drop on response terminal state, cancellation-safe)
- [ ] Timeout: Weak ref timer, cancel on new request, only fires when access count = 0
- [ ] Session timeout: configurable via --sessionTimeout flag
- [ ] Max sessions (1024 default): return 503 when limit reached
- [ ] Session ID: uuid::Uuid::new_v4(), cryptographically random
- [ ] Global memory semaphore integration: check on session creation
- [ ] Cleanup callback: invoked when session transitions to Closed
- [ ] Session stored inside Asupersync Region — Region cancel kills child
- [ ] Unit tests with LabRuntime (deterministic virtual time): timeout fire, timeout cancel, concurrent inc/dec, clear during timeout, max sessions, state machine transitions, DELETE idempotency, concurrent POST+DELETE race

## Quality Gates
- `cargo +nightly build --release` — no warnings
- `cargo +nightly test` — all tests pass
- `cargo +nightly clippy -- -D warnings` — no warnings
notes:
REGION PER SESSION: Create new Region per session via Region::new(). Spawn ChildBridge task inside this Region. When session transitions to Closed, call region.cancel() which cascades to ChildBridge kill sequence.

SESSION STORAGE: Use RwLock<HashMap<SessionId, Session>> (from std::sync). DashMap is optional optimization — only use if contention profiling shows RwLock bottleneck. DashMap is NOT in Cargo.toml deps; add if needed.

ACCESS GUARD LIFECYCLE: Move SessionAccessGuard into SSE stream state (field in SSE sender struct). When stream ends (client disconnect, error, close), guard is dropped and access count decremented. For non-streaming responses, guard lives in handler scope. Response finish/close events both trigger guard drop — use boolean guard to prevent double-decrement.

CLEANUP CALLBACK: Global callback registered at SessionManager construction (not per-session). Called once per session on Closed transition. Example use: gateway removes per-session HTTP/SSE state.

DELETE RESPONSE CODE: Use 200 OK (not 204). Verify against TS StreamableHTTPServerTransport source. Propagate same choice to US-010 and US-001b compat tests.

DELETE DRAIN IS ASYNC: DELETE /mcp returns 200 immediately. Session drain (waiting for in-flight requests) happens asynchronously. Do not block the DELETE response on drain completion. State transitions: Active→Closing(returns 503 for new requests)→Closed(returns 404). See D-015.

SESSION ID TYPE: Use a newtype SessionId(String) wrapping UUID v4 string. Implement Display, Hash, Eq. Avoids mixing up session IDs with other string parameters.

SESSION ACCESS COUNTER: Edge case — if a request arrives between counter reaching 0 and timeout firing, increment counter and cancel timeout. Use AtomicU64 with compare-and-swap for race-free decrement-and-check-zero.

title: US-007: CORS and Headers
description:
CORS middleware and custom header injection for all server modes.

## Tech Stack
- Asupersync middleware/layer pattern (per US-000 findings)
- `regex` crate for regex origin matching
- Applied to all HTTP routes in server modes

## File: src/cors.rs

## CORS Behavior (Three States)
1. `--cors` absent: No CORS headers at all. No OPTIONS handling.
2. `--cors` (present, no values): `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on all responses. Handle OPTIONS preflight with 204.
3. `--cors origin1 origin2`: Reflect `Origin` header if it's in the allowed list. Add `Vary: Origin` header. Handle OPTIONS preflight with 204.

## Origin Matching Types
Origins can be specified as:
- Exact string match: `https://example.com`
- Regex pattern: `/pattern/` syntax (compiled via regex crate, matched against request Origin)
- Wildcard: `*` (handled by AllowAll state above)

## CorsConfig
```rust
enum CorsConfig {
    Disabled,                              // --cors absent
    AllowAll,                              // --cors with no values
    AllowList(Vec<OriginMatcher>),         // --cors origin1 /regex/ origin2
}

enum OriginMatcher {
    Exact(String),
    Regex(regex::Regex),
}
```

## Credentials Safety
Wildcard origin (`*`) MUST NOT be combined with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials`. If credentials are needed, reflect the specific origin instead of using wildcard.

## Preflight Response (OPTIONS)
- Status: 204 No Content
- `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`: * or reflected origin
- `Access-Control-Allow-Methods`: GET, POST, DELETE, OPTIONS
- `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`: Content-Type, Accept, Mcp-Session-Id, Last-Event-ID
- `Access-Control-Expose-Headers`: Mcp-Session-Id (when stateful HTTP mode — set via `expose_session_header: bool`)
- `Access-Control-Max-Age`: 86400 (default, configurable)
- `Vary: Origin` (when using AllowList mode)

## Custom Headers (--header)
- Applied to ALL HTTP responses: SSE streams, POST responses, health/readiness, Streamable HTTP, error responses (4xx, 5xx)
- Injected after CORS headers
- Format parsed by CLI (US-002): Vec<(String, String)>
- Last-wins semantics for duplicate keys

## --oauth2Bearer
Adds `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header to outgoing requests. Only when token is actually provided (non-empty). Fixes TS bug D-003.

## Max Header Size
Max 8KB per header. Reject oversized headers with HTTP 431 Request Header Fields Too Large.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CorsConfig enum with three states (Disabled, AllowAll, AllowList)
- [ ] Origin matching: exact strings AND regex patterns (/pattern/ syntax)
- [ ] CORS disabled: no CORS headers, no OPTIONS handling
- [ ] CORS AllowAll: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on all responses
- [ ] CORS AllowList: reflect Origin if matched (exact or regex), add `Vary: Origin`
- [ ] Credentials safety: wildcard origin NOT combined with Allow-Credentials
- [ ] OPTIONS preflight: 204 with Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers, Expose-Headers, Max-Age
- [ ] Expose-Headers includes Mcp-Session-Id when `expose_session_header` is true
- [ ] Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400 default
- [ ] --oauth2Bearer: adds Authorization header only when provided with non-empty value
- [ ] Max header size: 8KB, reject with 431
- [ ] Custom --header values applied to ALL responses (SSE, POST, health, errors)
- [ ] Middleware composable with Asupersync's web framework
- [ ] Unit tests: CORS disabled, wildcard, exact origin, regex origin, credentials edge case, OPTIONS preflight, custom headers, Vary: Origin, max header size, 431 rejection

## Quality Gates
- `cargo +nightly build --release` — no warnings
- `cargo +nightly test` — all tests pass
- `cargo +nightly clippy -- -D warnings` — no warnings
notes:
CUSTOM HEADERS SCOPE (TS PARITY): In TS, custom headers are NOT applied uniformly:
- SSE mode: headers on health, SSE connect response, and POST 202 responses
- Streamable HTTP modes: headers ONLY on health endpoint (NOT on POST/GET/DELETE MCP responses)
- WS mode: no custom headers at all (D-105 improvement adds them)
- Client modes: headers as outgoing request headers
Rust must match this per-mode scoping exactly for compat, OR document broader application as intentional improvement in DIVERGENCES.md.

HEALTH CONTENT-TYPE: TS Express res.send('ok') returns Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8 (Express default). Rust will likely return text/plain. Document as accepted divergence or match exactly.

title: US-008: Logging and Observability
description:
Logging infrastructure and observability counters for all modes.

## Tech Stack
- Custom Logger struct wrapping stderr/stdout routing
- AtomicU64 counters for all metrics
- No external logging framework (keep deps minimal)

## Files: src/observe.rs (counters), src/logger.rs (logging)

## Log Routing
- When outputTransport is `stdio`: ALL logs go to stderr (stdout is the MCP transport)
- Otherwise: info to stdout, error to stderr
- Wrap in a Logger struct that routes based on configured output transport

## Log Format
All messages prefixed with `[supergateway]`:
- `[supergateway] [LEVEL] message`
- The `[supergateway]` prefix is REQUIRED on all log messages (PRD line 339)

## Log Levels
- `debug`: verbose protocol messages, full JSON-RPC message bodies, counter snapshots, header values
- `info`: startup, connections, mode transitions, counter changes. NEVER log header values or full payloads at info level (security: may contain auth tokens)
- `none`: suppress all output

## Startup Message
On startup, log at info level (format may differ from TS — intentional divergence):
```
[supergateway] supergateway v<version> | <inputMode> → <outputMode> | port <port>
```

## Counter API
```rust
pub struct Metrics {
    pub active_sessions: AtomicU64,
    pub active_children: AtomicU64,
    pub active_clients: AtomicU64,
    pub total_requests: AtomicU64,
    pub total_spawns: AtomicU64,
    pub spawn_failures: AtomicU64,
    pub session_timeouts: AtomicU64,
    pub forced_kills: AtomicU64,
    pub client_disconnects: AtomicU64,
    pub backpressure_events: AtomicU64,
    pub queue_depth_max: AtomicU64,
    pub decode_errors: AtomicU64,
}
```
Shared as `Arc<Metrics>` across all gateway tasks.

## Counter Change Logging
Gauge counters (active_sessions, active_children, active_clients) logged at **info level** on change (PRD line 271). Use `inc_and_log()` / `dec_and_log()` wrappers.

## Health Endpoint
- `GET <configured-path>`: returns "ok" (plain text) when ready, 503 when not ready
- Readiness conditions: child started (server modes), transport bound, relay loop active
- `GET <configured-path>?detail=true` with `--logLevel debug`: returns JSON metrics snapshot (Content-Type: application/json)
- Without debug or ?detail: plain "ok" text

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Logger struct with stderr/stdout routing based on output transport
- [ ] All messages prefixed with `[supergateway]`
- [ ] Log levels: debug, info, none
- [ ] No header values at info level (security — only at debug)
- [ ] No full JSON-RPC payloads at info level (only method names and IDs)
- [ ] Startup message with version, mode, port
- [ ] Metrics struct with all counters as AtomicU64
- [ ] `inc_and_log()` / `dec_and_log()` wrappers logging at info level on gauge change
- [ ] Arc<Metrics> shared across tasks
- [ ] Health readiness gating: 503 until child started + transport bound + relay active
- [ ] Health detail: JSON metrics snapshot when debug + ?detail=true
- [ ] Test: logger routes to stderr when output=stdio
- [ ] Test: metrics increment/decrement/snapshot
- [ ] Test: health returns 503 before ready, 200 after
- [ ] Test: health detail returns JSON with all counter fields
- [ ] Test: no header values in info-level log output

## Quality Gates
- `cargo +nightly build --release` — no warnings
- `cargo +nightly test` — all tests pass
- `cargo +nightly clippy -- -D warnings` — no warnings
notes:
READINESS STATE: Add ready: AtomicBool to Metrics. Set to true when ALL conditions met. Health endpoint checks ready.load(Relaxed).
Per-mode readiness conditions:
- Server modes (SSE, WS, HTTP): child spawned successfully AND server listening on port
- Client modes (SSE->stdio, HTTP->stdio): connected to remote AND relay loop active
"Relay loop active" means: stdin reader started AND remote connection established.

LOGGER STDERR ROUTING: When outputTransport is "stdio", ALL log output (info and error) goes to stderr to avoid contaminating stdout (the JSON-RPC transport). This is TS behavior (getLogger.ts:66-83).

DEBUG FORMAT: debug logLevel should pretty-print object arguments with deep inspection. Color output if stderr is a TTY (check isatty).

INC/DEC WRAPPERS: Pass Logger as parameter: fn inc_and_log(counter: &AtomicU64, name: &str, logger: &Logger). Or make wrappers methods on a struct holding both Metrics and Logger reference.

READY GATE API: Expose set_ready() on health module. Called by each gateway mode after child process starts AND transport is accepting connections. Until set_ready() called, health returns 503. See D-103.

LOG FORMAT: Match TS format: no level tag in output. TS uses console.log/error directly. Rust logger should output plain messages (with optional timestamp prefix configurable via --logLevel). Debug level enables verbose output including message payloads.

LOG ROUTING: Logger output destination depends on outputTransport === 'stdio' (not input mode). When output is stdio, logs go to stderr to avoid corrupting the JSON-RPC stream. See TS getLogger.ts:77-82.

HEALTH DETAIL GATE: Observability detail endpoint (?detail=true) is gated on --logLevel debug. When logLevel is not debug, detail query parameter is ignored and only 'ok'/503 is returned.

COUNTER RATE LIMIT: Consider rate-limiting counter-change logs to avoid spamming under high throughput. TS has no rate limit but also logs less frequently due to event loop batching.
